description:
To properly set the group owner of /etc/security/opasswd, run the command: 
$ sudo chgrp root /etc/security/opasswd
rationale:
The <tt>/etc/security/opasswd</tt> file stores old passwords to prevent
password reuse. Protection of this file is critical for system security.
ocil_clause:
/etc/security/opasswd does not have a group owner of
root
ocil:
To check the group ownership of /etc/security/opasswd, run the command: 
$ ls -lL /etc/security/opasswd
If properly configured, the output should indicate the following group-owner: root
fixtext:
Change the group of the file /etc/security/opasswd to root by running the following command:
$ sudo chgrp root /etc/security/opasswd
srg_requirement: The Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 /etc/security/opasswd file must be group-owned by root.
title: Verify Group Who Owns /etc/security/opasswd File
